mechanism: Collect linguistic data (surnames vs. full names used in reference) from multiple sources (online reviews, radio discussions, self-reported speech). Compare frequency of surname-only reference between male and female professionals across domains using contingency analysis. Conduct experimental manipulation: randomly assign a described professional's gender while holding all other attributes constant, then measure resulting referencing patterns. Analyze downstream consequences by having independent raters judge eminence and deservingness of awards based on referencing style, using between-group comparison.
domain: social psychology, gender bias, language use

mechanism: Across multiple domains (peer evaluation, relationship judgment, product evaluation), elicit participants' predictions of how much information they and others will examine before forming an opinion. Collect actual behavior data measuring information consumption before decision. Compute the ratio of predicted to actual information examined. Conduct repeated measurements within subjects across different evaluation targets. Apply meta-analytical aggregation across studies to identify systematic discrepancy patterns.

Record psychophysiological response signals (heart rate, skin conductance) while presenting a set of news video stimuli. Classify each stimulus as having negative, neutral, or positive valence. Partition response data by stimulus valence. Compute mean response magnitude and variability within each partition. Test for significant differences in response magnitude between partitions using repeated-measures statistical comparison. Examine heterogeneity in response patterns across individual participants and across geopolitical groups.

For each respondent, identify and categorize relationship types (family, friends, work, etc.) they engage in. Compute a diversity index (richness and evenness) over the distribution of time spent across relationship types. Measure well-being using standardized instruments. Regress well-being against diversity index while controlling for total social interaction time and activity diversity. Execute specification curve analysis to test robustness across alternative definitions of diversity, well-being metrics, and covariate sets. Apply the same regression framework within individual over time using longitudinal data.

Experimentally manipulate mental salience (cue thoughts of God vs. control). Present recommendation scenarios across multiple domains. Measure willingness to follow AI recommendation vs. human recommendation using rating scales. Test mediation pathway: God salience → feeling of smallness → reduced reliance on human judgment. Measure individual attributes (country, financial decision context) as moderators. Estimate effect sizes and confidence intervals. Conduct multi-level aggregation across country-level data for robo-advisor adoption.

Extract a large set of sentence tokens from a corpus, each containing a gerund or infinitival complement construction. Identify the main verb in each token. Compute a distinctive collexeme analysis matrix: rows are verbs, columns are construction types, entries are association strengths. Rank verbs by their discriminative power for each construction. Use ranked verbs as stimuli in controlled experiments. In sentence completion, measure construction choice (gerund vs. infinitive) after prior completion or rating of sentences. Apply log-linear models or contingency analysis to detect short-range and accumulative priming effects. In rating tasks, analyze acceptability scores using linear regression against collexemic properties.
domain: second language acquisition, construction grammar, psycholinguistics

mechanism: Record audio samples of speakers (L1 English and L1 Tagalog speakers of English). Independently vary speaker identity, job prestige level (high/low), and performance level (high/low). Recruit listeners to rate each audio along multiple dimensions (competence, treatment preference, comprehensibility) using ordinal rating scales. Compute mean ratings within each condition and speaker group. Perform factorial analysis of variance to partition variance by speaker language background, job prestige, performance level, and their interactions.

Assemble a corpus of L2 English texts produced by L1 Spanish learners at four proficiency levels (A1–B2). Manually annotate morpheme instances with error codes, identifying accuracy for each morpheme category. Tabulate accuracy counts and error subtypes for each morpheme at each proficiency level. Compute accuracy order (rank morphemes by accuracy percentage within each level). Compare observed order against reference data from prior Morpheme Order Studies. Analyze variation in order by proficiency level and error subtype using descriptive and inferential aggregation.
domain: second language acquisition, learner corpora, morphosyntax

mechanism: Measure perceptions of financial security (whether one can afford needs) and perceived life meaning using survey items. Test bivariate association between financial security and meaning using correlation or simple regression. Regress meaning on security while including income as covariate to assess whether security explains outcome variance beyond income. Experimentally manipulate financial security threat (present scenarios undermining security perception). Measure induced change in meaning perception. Apply regression framework to longitudinal data to examine within-person changes.

Present visual scenes: an emotional or neutral foreground object on a neutral or varied background. Collect free recall of the object and background at test. Alternatively, provide one scene element as a retrieval cue and measure recall of the associated element. Compute recall accuracy and conditional probabilities: probability of recalling background given that object is cued, and vice versa. Compare these probabilities between emotional and neutral object conditions. Analyze whether emotional items are more likely to trigger contextual recall than neutral items.

Digitally manipulate face images: vary facial redness (incrementally), expression (neutral vs. angry), and masculinity (face shape morphing). Create a factorial stimulus set. Recruit raters to judge perceived anger and health for each face image. Model relationships using linear or polynomial regression of anger/health ratings against redness level. Test moderation: examine whether effects of redness differ by expression type and masculine features using interaction terms. Compute effect sizes and plot fitted response curves.
domain: social psychology, emotion perception, facial cues

mechanism: Select photographs of disgusting and frightening scenarios, matched on dimensions of emotional valence, arousal, and relatedness (measured via Latent Semantic Analysis on item descriptions). Present sets of photographs to participants for study. Measure free recall of which photographs were shown. Compute recall rate by emotion category. Measure attention during encoding via eye-tracking or explicit attention allocation. Model recall as a function of emotion category and attention, using regression. Test mediation: whether emotional category affects recall primarily through attention.

Present threat, happy, and neutral face stimuli at two exposure durations (500 and 1250 milliseconds). Use a dot probe task: after stimulus presentation, a probe appears in the location of one face or neutral space. Measure reaction time to detect the probe. Compute attentional bias index: difference in reaction time between congruent (probe at face) and incongruent (probe away) trials. Compare bias indices across stimulus types and durations using between-group and within-subject contrasts (high vs. low trait anxiety). Test for modulation of bias by dysphoria.

Present written behavioral scenarios describing actions with varying moral valence (good vs. bad). For each scenario, ask participants to judge whether the action is intentional. Partition scenarios by moral quality and outcome (intended vs. unintended). Estimate the proportion of 'intentional' judgments within each partition. Test whether moral valence significantly predicts intentionality judgment using contingency analysis or logistic regression. Control for alternative factors (outcome, effort) using multivariate methods.

For each participant, assess endorsement of moral nonobjectivism (disagreement about moral facts) using survey items. Separately administer the moral/conventional task: present violations and ask whether the act would be wrong in an alternate context, and whether wrongness is culture-dependent. Compare participants who endorsed nonobjectivist claims with those who rejected them on the distribution of responses in the moral/conventional task. Test whether the two groups differ in their boundary between moral and conventional violations using contingency analysis.

Present written scenarios describing actions with varying levels of moral features (praiseworthy vs. blameworthy), skill requirement (high vs. low), luck (high vs. low outcome control), and outcome. Ask participants to judge whether the action is intentional. Analyze judgment responses using multivariate logistic regression with predictors: moral features, skill level, luck level, and their interactions. Test which factors significantly predict intentionality judgment. Compare effect magnitudes across predictor types to assess relative importance.

Develop written scenarios describing agents in deterministic or indeterministic environments performing actions. Vary whether the agent's past causally necessitates their choice. Ask participants whether the agent acts freely and is morally responsible. Collect binary or ordinal responses. Estimate proportion endorsing free will and responsibility judgments. Test whether endorsed free will differs between deterministic and indeterministic scenarios using contingency analysis. Summarize folk intuitions across scenarios.
domain: experimental philosophy, free will, moral responsibility

mechanism: Present scenarios describing an agent with conflicting goals and desires regarding some object (e.g., desiring both to achieve a good outcome and to avoid a bad outcome). Vary the moral goodness of the object. Ask participants to attribute desires and moral beliefs to the agent. Measure attributions using ordinal or binary response scales. Test whether moral goodness of the object predicts attributed valence of desires and moral beliefs using regression or contingency analysis. Propose an alternative interpretation based on egocentric biases in third-person mind attribution.
domain: experimental philosophy, folk psychology, mind attribution

mechanism: Present written vignettes describing an agent performing an action that causes an outcome. Vary moral valence of the action and atypicality of the agent's behavior relative to baseline expectations. Ask participants to judge whether the agent caused the outcome. Collect binary or continuous causality judgments. Fit regression models with predictors: moral valence, atypicality, and interaction term. Compare effect magnitudes. Test whether atypicality independently contributes to causality judgment beyond moral considerations.

Administer variants of Nozick's experience machine thought-experiment, manipulating ease of switching from real to virtual life (status quo bias manipulation). Measure willingness to plug into a virtual-reality machine. Collect binary or ordinal responses. Compare endorsement rates across variant framings. Fit a status quo bias model to explain choice behavior. Test whether reduced switching willingness persists regardless of reality vs. simulation framing, suggesting status quo effects rather than reality valuation. Estimate effect sizes for bias components.
